meta.title: soma

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Batch 2: transcribed from the owner's roots (Greek and Latin) reference; 2 taught, 2 reach.

root.label: soma
root.mentor_note: soma keeps every word it builds anchored to the physical body, not the mind.

attempt.direction_prompt: Only in the mind, or showing in the body too?

context_sentences: The therapist asked her to describe the somatic symptoms first: the tight chest, the shallow breath.
Years of stress had produced clear somatic effects, long before he admitted anything was wrong.
After every test came back clear, the doctor gently suggested the pain might be psychosomatic.
His psychosomatic headaches always arrived on Sunday nights, just before the working week began.
Early somatology laid the groundwork for what later became modern human anatomy.
His lectures on somatology were dense, but nobody who attended forgot the diagrams.

construct_options.text: The scientific study of the structure and function of the human body
The study of dreams and the unconscious mind
The study of ancient burial practices

context_sentences: The coach designed each training plan around the athlete’s somatotype rather than a single generic routine.
Old fitness science leaned heavily on somatotype, sorting people into just three broad body types.

construct_options.text: A classification of a person’s physical body type
A style of clothing designed for athletes
A classification of personality and temperament
